dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 for a seizure call at Broadview Apartments.
Hello, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue team. What brought you to call 911 today?
She had a big seizure, worse than usual-laid down for a while now.
Be careful folks, I'll touch her shoulder gently, is she moving or reactive at all?
Attempting verbal response-Ma'am, can you hear me? What's your name?
Testing motor response-lightly touch the patient here.
She pulls her arm slightly-reactive to mild pain and stimulus.
Airway appears unobstructed. Any signs of abnormal sounds or blockage?
No signs of airway obstruction. Applying nasal capnography for confirmation.
Capnography indicates a regular waveform-airway is clear and unobstructed.
Breathing is at an adequate rate and depth. Observe signs of effort or distress.
No signs of labored breathing. Respiratory rate-18 per minute. Oximetry is 95%.
Confirmed-18 breaths per minute.
Pulse is 77, strong and regular. Skin is warm and dry-no cyanosis observed.
Blood pressure is 118/68-no acute signs of shock or fluid deficiency.
Blood pressure is 118 over 68.
Bystander, can you explain the events leading up to this seizure and her current condition?
She told me that she fell this morning and hurt her face, then I found her seizing.
Has there been any change in her medications recently?
She was out of her Dilantin for at least a week. Prescribed cephalexin, but hasn't picked it up yet.
When exactly did she contact you this morning?
She called me at 6:30 a.m. and stayed down. I arrived to find her seizing-can't say how long.
She is currently non-responsive-pain level is noted as 3 by EMS providers during observation.
Inspecting skin for swelling or wounds.
She has a cut on her upper lip and a big bruise on her nose.
Laceration is minor, currently under control with direct pressure and gauze.
Applied direct pressure on lip wound with a 4x4 bandage-bleeding is controlled.
Can you see any signs of trauma to the head or neck?
Pupils are equal and reactive. Trachea is midline.
No jugular venous distension observed.
Check breath sounds-any abnormalities?
Clear breath sounds in all lung fields-no adventitious sounds.
Chest wall movement is intact and symmetrical. No deformity or tenderness noted.
Press gently to assess-abdomen is soft, non-tender, and non-distended.
Pelvic assessment is stable-no instability or pain on palpation.
+PMSx4-all four extremities intact.
Pulse is 77 beats per minute and regular.
Let's check blood sugar level-plenty of time and tools.
Blood sugar is at 0 mg/dL. May be influenced by the post-seizure state.
Oxygen saturation is 95%-will monitor.
SpO2 improved slightly to 96%-likely due to airway clearance.
Starting 4-lead ECG now-monitoring for tachycardia or arrhythmias.
Confirming normal sinus tachycardia as per capnography and chest monitoring.
Administering 4 liters per minute via nasal cannula for supportive oxygen.
PERRLA noted-pupils equally round, reactive to light and accommodation.
Respiratory rate is at 16-breathing is controlled, no distress.
Updated BP-130 over 79. Increase from initial, but not concerning at this time.
Tachycardia noted-pulse at 124 BPM with no known chest pain reported.
Blood glucose is now 125-continuing trend shows possible improvement.
Attempting vein access in an extremity to establish IV per protocol.
IV attempted in left forearm-unsuccessful due to patient movement.
Attempted right arm-also unsuccessful. Attempts marked as unsuccessful.
No medication administered on scene outside of supplemental oxygen.
Based on seizure history and vitals, following medical seizure protocol 3-12.
Taking to [COMPANY_NAME], our designated seizure center per written plan.
Moving her onto the stretcher-placing in semi-Fowler's to assist breathing.
Secured with five seat belts for transport in a stable position.
On arrival, assist to ER bed with draw sheet and full restraints as needed.
Oropharynx suctioned and passed to ER team-hand-off with [NAME], RN.
A second Grand Mal seizure occurred en route to the ER-no new meds given.
Seizure ceased within 30 seconds without medication-patient stabilized.
Double-checked latest vitals-pulse at 124, BP 130/79, SpO2 96%.
Respiratory rate is 16-no signs of worsening distress.
Glucose is up to 125 mg/dL-changing, but not urgent at the moment.
No new protocols needed at this time-Hospital management advised.
Patient with known Epilepsy, Bronchitis, and Emphysema.
Current meds include Sucralfate, phenytoin, Methadone, Zoloft, Vistaril, and cephalexin.
No known drug allergies-documented as per patient records.
Delivered safely to ER. All actions done per protocol-no incidents reported.
Negative for JVD-no swelling or distention at all.
Repeat of BP: 130/79-no change indicative of shock or other issues.
Tachycardia is consistent-assessed as related to post-seizure state and conditions.
Soft and stable-free from signs of internal bleeding or trauma.
Pelvis exam shows no crepitus or instability-fracture unlikely.
Re-checked neck-still midline and head injury evaluated as minor.
No further interventions conducted-vitals stable with current practices.
Pain level remains at 3 per assessment-no reported shift or increase.
Pain documented consistently during transfer and through second episode.
Monitoring continues-SpO2 maintained at 96% during second event.
Blood sugar test again-125 mg/dL-within acceptable non-critical post-seizure range.
Destination still appropriately set to [COMPANY_NAME].
Comfort secured for her during transport with space for medical use.
Requested assistance at hospital entrance-prepared transfer for bed or stretcher.
No abnormal color or signs of voiding issues-stable renal function.
Skin remains intact and warm except for minor laceration on upper lip.
Recheck IV status-still unsuccessful-documented in patient records.
Still shows sinus tach-ECG unchanged since start of episode and up to handoff.
All actions justified for protocol adherence-no breaches or concerns.
No changes to care-procedures unchanged and continued as before.
Hospital focus remains [CITY], given neurological condition and prior protocols.
All transport procedures used-no loss of stability or safety incidents.
Reconfirms baseline-no missing data points in care documentation.
Medication history clear-None held, and no new prescriptions at this moment.
ER team fully briefed and patients condition discussed with RN on shifts.
Documented that patient unable to consent at time of review for legal records.
Transportation complete-no significant changes after second seizure event.
No further actions chosen-decision arrived at prehospital protocol threshold.
